docs.title: Priv - Online Anonymity Guide
docs.text: Welcome to Priv, your comprehensive resource for learning about online anonymity and finding the best tools to protect your digital footprint.

docs.title: What is Online Anonymity?
docs.text: Online anonymity is the ability to interact on the internet without revealing your real-world identity. Unlike privacy, which is about controlling what information others can see about you, anonymity is about concealing who you are entirely.

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text:
• Getting Started: Introduction to anonymity concepts and threat modeling
  • Tools: Curated selection of privacy-focused tools and services
  • Concepts: Core principles of privacy, security, and anonymity
  • News: Latest updates in the privacy and anonymity space

Start exploring by selecting a section from the navigation menu on the left.

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Understanding the difference between privacy, security, and anonymity is essential for practicing operational security (OPSEC).

docs.title: Privacy vs. Security vs. Anonymity
docs.text:
• Privacy: Control over your data and who sees it
  • Security: Protection of your systems and data from unauthorized access
  • Anonymity: Concealing your identity entirely

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Even if your message contents are encrypted, metadata (who you talk to, when, from where, and for how long) can still leak your identity. True anonymity requires metadata minimization.

docs.title: Threat Modeling
docs.text: A threat model is a process by which you identify potential security threats and define countermeasures: - Who are you hiding from? - What assets are you protecting? - What are the consequences of a leak?

docs.title: Encryption
docs.text:
Understand different types of encryption and when they're appropriate: - Symmetric encryption - Asymmetric encryption - End-to-end encryption

Expand on cryptography, onion routing, browser fingerprinting, and advanced threat modeling in the detailed concept guides.

docs.title: Privacy vs. Anonymity
docs.text:
Privacy means controlling what information about yourself is visible to others: - Example: Using encrypted messaging so only the recipient can read your messages - Focus: What information is disclosed

Anonymity means concealing your identity entirely: - Example: Browsing a forum without anyone knowing who you actually are - Focus: Who you are

docs.title: Metadata and Its Importance
docs.text:
Metadata is information about your communications rather than the communications themselves:

  • Who: Identifies of communication parties
  • When: Timing of communications
  • Where: Location and source/destination addresses
  • How: Duration and type of communication

Even with perfect encryption, metadata leaks can reveal sensitive information about your activities and relationships.

docs.title: Threat Modeling Process
docs.text:
Effective protection starts with understanding your specific threats:

  1. Identify your adversary: Who specifically are you protecting against?
  2. Define your assets: What needs protection?
  3. Assess consequences: What happens if something is compromised?
  4. Choose countermeasures: Select tools and practices matching your threat level

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Explore specific concepts in detail or review recommended tools and operational security practices.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to the Anonymity Guide! This space is dedicated to learning about online anonymity and finding the best open-source and paid tools to protect your digital footprint.

docs.title: What is Online Anonymity?
docs.text: Online anonymity is the ability to interact on the internet without revealing your real-world identity. Unlike privacy (which is about controlling what information others can see about you), anonymity is about concealing who you are.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
In this section, we will walk you through:

  1. Threat Modeling: Understanding your specific risk profile.
  2. Basic Hygiene: Small changes that yield massive privacy gains.
  3. Advanced Setup: Stepping up your anonymity game with operational security (OPSEC).

Expand the sections below to learn more about each topic.

docs.title: Introduction to Online Anonymity
docs.text: This introductory guide covers the fundamentals of online anonymity and why it matters in today's digital world.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Privacy vs. Anonymity: Privacy controls what information is visible. Anonymity conceals who you are.
  • Metadata: Information about your communications (who, when, where) can leak your identity even if message contents are encrypted.
  • Threat Model: Understanding who you're hiding from and what you're protecting helps you choose appropriate tools and practices.

docs.title: Why Anonymity Matters
docs.text:
In an era of widespread surveillance, data breaches, and digital tracking, online anonymity provides:

  • Protection from targeted attacks
  • Freedom of expression without fear of retaliation
  • Privacy from corporate data collection
  • Security against state-level surveillance

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Review the core concepts, explore available tools, and learn best practices for maintaining your anonymity online.

docs.title: News & Updates
docs.text: Stay informed on the latest developments in the privacy and anonymity space. This section catalogs relevant news, tool audits, and threat landscape updates.

docs.title: Latest Updates
docs.text:
• Security Audits: Summaries of independent code audits of popular VPNs and encrypted messaging services.
  • Vulnerabilities: Important alerts regarding browser exploits, zero-days, or configuration leaks.
  • Regulatory News: Updates on encryption bans, surveillance laws, and censorship developments around the world.

docs.title: Staying Informed
docs.text:
Following privacy and security news helps you: - Stay aware of new threats and vulnerabilities - Learn about emerging tools and technologies - Understand changing regulatory landscapes - Connect with the privacy community

Keep this page updated with links to latest news articles, RSS feeds, or your own blog posts regarding the state of online anonymity!

docs.title: Recent Developments
docs.text: This page tracks important news and developments in the privacy and anonymity space.

docs.title: 🔒 Security Audits
docs.text: Independent security audits of privacy tools help identify vulnerabilities and validate security claims. Regular audits are a sign of a trustworthy tool.

docs.title: ⚠️ Vulnerabilities
docs.text: Zero-day exploits, browser vulnerabilities, and configuration leaks are critical security issues that can compromise anonymity. Staying informed allows you to respond quickly.

docs.title: ⚖️ Regulatory News
docs.text: Governments worldwide are implementing policies that affect encryption, privacy, and surveillance. Understanding these developments helps you adapt your practices.

docs.title: 📰 Industry News
docs.text: New tools, protocol improvements, and community developments shape the privacy landscape.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Have news to share? Add important updates, links, and summaries to keep this community informed!

docs.title: Tools for Online Anonymity
docs.text: Choosing the right tools is critical to maintaining online anonymity. Here, we categorize tools into Open Source (FOSS) and Paid Services.

docs.title: 🌐 Browsers & Operating Systems
docs.text:
• Tor Browser: The gold standard for anonymous web browsing.
  • Tails OS: A live operating system that runs from a USB stick and routes all traffic through Tor.
  • Qubes OS: A security-focused operating system that uses virtualization to isolate different activities.

docs.title: 🛡️ VPNs & Proxies
docs.text:
• Mullvad VPN: A privacy-focused VPN that doesn't require an email address or personal details to register.
  • ProtonVPN: A reputable VPN service based in Switzerland with a strong focus on security.

docs.title: 💬 Encrypted Communication
docs.text:
• Signal: End-to-end encrypted messaging.
  • Session: An onion-routed, metadata-free messenger that does not require phone numbers.

docs.title: Choosing Your Tools
docs.text:
The best tools depend on your threat model and use case. No single tool provides complete anonymity—layering multiple tools based on your needs is often the most effective approach.

Add your detailed tool guides, configuration instructions, and security audits here!

docs.title: Tor Browser
docs.text: The most widely used tool for anonymous web browsing. Routes traffic through multiple nodes to conceal your location and identity.

docs.title: VPN Services
docs.text: Virtual Private Networks encrypt your traffic and mask your IP address. Choose between privacy-focused services like Mullvad VPN or ProtonVPN.

docs.title: Encrypted Messaging
docs.text: Applications like Signal and Session provide end-to-end encryption for communications without requiring phone numbers or other personal identifiers.

docs.title: Operating Systems
docs.text:
Tails OS and Qubes OS provide enhanced privacy and security at the operating system level:

  • Tails: Leave no digital footprint with this amnesic incognito live system
  • Qubes: Use virtualization to compartmentalize activities and isolate sensitive work

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Layer your tools: Use multiple tools together for better protection
  2. Understand your threat model: Choose tools appropriate for your specific risks
  3. Keep software updated: Security patches are critical
  4. Practice operational security: Tools are only effective when used properly

More detailed guides coming soon!
